title: Jazzagals

jokes.text: Moira mistakes David for Alexis, claiming she had a lengthy conversation with 'Alexis' about hosiery and menopause
jokes.setup: Moira complains that Alexis isn't ready; David points out Alexis hasn't been there for a week and a half
jokes.punchline: Moira insists she had breakfast with Alexis yesterday — 'We had a lengthy conversation about hosiery and menopause' — and David reveals that was him
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully layered cold-open joke. The escalation is what makes it exceptional: first Moira doesn't notice Alexis is gone, then she claims she had breakfast with her, then — the killer — 'We had a lengthy conversation about hosiery and menopause.' The content of the conversation (topics that could plausibly apply to both Alexis and David in Moira's oblivious mind) is perfectly chosen. It's deeply character-integrated: only Moira is self-absorbed enough to not notice she's talking to her son, and only David would have this specific reaction. Earns an 8 because the joke builds in three distinct beats and the specificity of 'hosiery and menopause' is genuinely inspired.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Do you know what moths do to cashmere? They don't just eat it, they mock it while they're eating it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, that's the price you pay for having nice things.

jokes.text: The moth / triple-locked titanium suitcase / cashmere exchange
jokes.setup: Moira asks why David isn't ready; he complains about a moth in a triple-locked titanium suitcase
jokes.punchline: David deadpans: 'The perils of owning cashmere'
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of a moth penetrating a 'triple-locked titanium suitcase' paired with David's world-weary, aristocratic response — 'The perils of owning cashmere' — is efficient and perfectly in character. It also seeds the episode's cedar chest storyline. The economy is excellent; it does a lot in very few words.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I had breakfast with Alexis yesterday.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, that was me.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, it was Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, I was there. It was me. You had breakfast with me.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, you were dressed so similarly.

jokes.text: Patrick is behind the desk at the garage but clarifies he doesn't 'work here' in the garage — then immediately contradicts himself
jokes.setup: A customer asks Patrick for help with his muffler because Patrick is standing behind the desk
jokes.punchline: Patrick fumbles through: 'I work here, but I don't actually work here... in the garage. I mean, I am working here in the garage, but I don't work for the garage.'
jokes.explanation: A solid fish-out-of-water cringe moment. Patrick (presumably) is in an awkward professional limbo and the verbal loop he gets trapped in is recognizable and relatable. Functional character comedy that establishes Johnny's new situation, but not particularly original or memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, I had the most wonderful conversation with you earlier about the nutritional benefits of activated charcoal.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mother, I would never discuss activated charcoal with you. That sounds like something Alexis would do.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, you were very passionate about it. You said it was 'life-changing' and 'better than therapy.'

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I would never say that.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And then we moved on to discussing the proper way to contour one's décolletage, and you had very strong opinions about highlight placement.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay, that was definitely Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: But then you pivoted to cryptocurrency and explained blockchain technology using only hand gestures for twenty minutes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mother, no one has ever done that.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You seemed quite confident about it, darling.

jokes.text: The customer gives up and says he'll 'just go to another garage' after Patrick's extended non-explanation
jokes.setup: Patrick has spent nearly a minute failing to explain his status at the garage
jokes.punchline: The customer simply opts out — 'Okay, well, maybe I'll just... go to another garage' — as the logical conclusion to Patrick's absurdity
jokes.explanation: The straight-man customer's understated exit is the clean button on the fumbling exchange. Economy is strong — the line does exactly what it needs to. Not a sophisticated joke but well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I don't work here.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Then why are you standing behind the counter?

jokes.text: Johnny reveals he let a customer walk out rather than look at the muffler because 'I'm not a mechanic!'
jokes.setup: Bob returns with a muffin (comic timing on 'muffin' vs 'muffler'); Johnny reports a customer had a muffler problem
jokes.punchline: 'I don't know, I didn't go out and look! I'm not a mechanic!'
jokes.explanation: The muffin/muffler near-homophone is a nice bit of ambient wordplay, and Johnny's defensive 'I'm not a mechanic!' reveals his fish-out-of-water pride. The joke works because it's simultaneously correct (he isn't a mechanic) and absurdly unhelpful — classic Johnny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not exactly employed in the traditional sense. I'm more of a... consultant. A lifestyle consultant, if you will.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I advise people on matters of taste, aesthetics, and general refinement. Though technically, I'm not being paid at the moment.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: But I do have a business card. Several, actually. From my previous ventures. I just haven't updated them yet.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So while I'm between opportunities, I'm essentially exploring my passions and honing my craft in the retail sector. Part-time.

jokes.text: Bob's passive-aggressive jab: 'I know you're a little low on clients'
jokes.setup: Bob suggests he'd help Johnny's clients if they came in; Johnny pushes back
jokes.punchline: Bob twists the knife: 'Not that I have to worry, I know you're a little low on clients'
jokes.explanation: A competent sitcom burn — Bob is cheerfully devastating. Works in context as character comedy but not especially original or quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I went to that new bakery and got a muffin.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A muffler? Finally! I've been trying to get someone to look at that noise for weeks.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: No, a muffin. The baked good.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, I didn't help with that either.

jokes.text: Johnny's increasingly grandiose description of his 'strategy mode' for a business with zero clients
jokes.setup: Bob points out Johnny has no clients
jokes.punchline: Johnny responds with executive-speak: 'I'm in strategy mode, looking for opportunities, and eventually I will implement a plan'
jokes.explanation: Johnny retreating into the language of corporate consultancy while sitting in a rural garage with no clients is deeply character-specific. The gap between his self-image (powerful executive) and reality (man with zero clients in Bob's garage) is the engine of his storyline. Solidly above average character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I'd be happy to talk to your clients.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Please don't.

jokes.text: Bob's offer: 'If you feel like you might like to implement a muffler, be my guest'
jokes.setup: Johnny uses corporate language about 'implementing a plan'
jokes.punchline: Bob riffs on the word 'implement' by suggesting Johnny implement a muffler — returning to the earlier customer incident
jokes.explanation: Bob's callback that loops 'implement' from Johnny's corporate jargon back to the literal muffler job is elegant wordplay — it's economical, it punishes Johnny's pretension with a perfectly calibrated blue-collar retort, and it calls back the earlier joke. Bob's cheerful obliviousness makes it land even harder.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: You know, Johnny, in retail, the customer is always right. But in your case, I think they're just always... confused.

jokes.text: David knows cedar by smell because of a cologne 'supposed to smell like the aftermath of a car crashing into a cedar tree'
jokes.setup: Stevie asks how David knows the planks are cedar
jokes.punchline: David explains he bought a cologne in Japan designed to smell like the aftermath of a car crashing into a cedar tree
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode. The specificity is extraordinary: not just a cedar cologne, but one purchased in Japan, designed to evoke the aftermath of a car crashing into a cedar tree. Every word earns its place. It's absurdist without being random — it perfectly characterizes David's wealth, his pretension, and his complete obliviousness to how ridiculous he sounds. Stevie's follow-up ('Why would anyone want to smell like that?') is the perfect straight-man beat. Highly quotable and deeply character-specific. Justifies an 8.5 because it's a genuinely exceptional joke that would land in any context, not just for fans of the show.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: You don't have any clients.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I'm in a recalibration phase. I'm doing some deep-dive strategic planning, synergizing my core competencies, and leveraging my bandwidth to optimize my value proposition in a very competitive marketplace.

jokes.text: Stevie's reaction: 'Why would anyone wanna smell like that?' — then immediately drops it
jokes.setup: David explains the cedar-crash cologne
jokes.punchline: Stevie's baffled 'Why would anyone want to smell like that?' followed by an immediate pivot — 'You know what? Never mind, the cedar is yours'
jokes.explanation: Stevie's choice to simply give up and move on rather than engage with David's absurdity is a perfect character beat for her. The pivot ('You know what? Never mind') is the ideal straight-man response — she's learned that engaging with David rarely goes anywhere useful. Very economical.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: You know what, Johnny? I've been thinking about implementing a strategic initiative regarding your car's muffler situation.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I'm going to circle back with some solutions, touch base with a mechanic, and hopefully we can pivot on getting that fixed.

jokes.text: David describes the moth infestation as moths mistaking his cashmere for 'an Atlantic City all-you-can-eat buffet'
jokes.setup: Stevie asks what David plans to do with the cedar
jokes.punchline: David frames the moth damage with the specific metaphor of an Atlantic City all-you-can-eat buffet
jokes.explanation: The Atlantic City all-you-can-eat buffet is a well-chosen specific detail — it carries class connotations (downscale, excessive) that are perfect coming from David about something happening to his luxury items. It's a callback to the cold-open moth joke that deepens it.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What kind of wood is this?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a very specific blend of oak and cedar with top notes of bergamot and a hint of... is that nutmeg?

jokes.text: Stevie's skeptical repetition — 'You're planning on building a cedar chest? You are. You're gonna build it?'
jokes.setup: David announces he's building a cedar chest
jokes.punchline: Stevie repeats the claim in three different forms, each more incredulous, as if waiting for him to correct himself
jokes.explanation: The escalating repetition — 'You're planning on building... You are. You're gonna build it?' — is a great use of the pause/repetition structure. Stevie's disbelief is perfectly calibrated to David's character (someone who has never built anything in his life). 'It's a box!' is David's defensive non-answer, which only makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So what does it smell like?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's not about what it smells like. It's about what it makes you feel like.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Okay, so what does it make you feel like?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know, like... expensive.

jokes.text: 'May the force be with you' — Stevie's send-off to David's woodworking project
jokes.setup: David has earnestly thanked Stevie multiple times for the cedar
jokes.punchline: Stevie caps it with a deadpan 'May the force be with you' — sincere in tone, devastating in implication
jokes.explanation: Stevie's use of a pop culture sign-off to imply David is about to attempt something impossible is good Stevie characterization. The follow-through — David's sincere 'Thank you so much. Thank you, genuinely. Honestly, you're so great.' — makes it even better by showing David completely misses the sarcasm.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The moths have gotten into the cashmere. It's like a casino buffet for them — they're just gorging themselves on my collection.

jokes.text: Moira hails Twyla twice loudly while Twyla is clearly in the middle of something with other customers
jokes.setup: Moira enters the café and sees Twyla with other customers
jokes.punchline: Moira yells 'Twyla! Twyla!' as if the social norms of waiting don't apply to her
jokes.explanation: Standard Moira entitlement behavior — functional character comedy. Not a standout joke on its own but establishes the café scene and Moira's oblivious self-importance.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You're going to build that yourself?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I've seen it done. It's just nailing planks together.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Right. Just nailing planks together.

jokes.text: Moira's passive-aggressive 'no big ladies' bruncheon for me' fishing for an invitation
jokes.setup: Moira sees the Jazzagals having breakfast together; Twyla asks if she's waiting for a friend
jokes.punchline: Moira: 'Just an intimate breakfast, nothing special, no big ladies' bruncheon for me' — transparently angling for inclusion
jokes.explanation: Moira's transparent fishing for an invitation — wrapped in the veneer of aristocratic indifference — is pitch-perfect character comedy. The invented term 'ladies' bruncheon' is specifically Moira, and her escalating qualification ('whatever that was, a gathering of the girls') is brilliantly evasive. The joke works because we can see exactly what she wants.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: May the Force be with you.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank you. That's very sweet.

jokes.text: Moira mocks the Jazzagals' New York trip — 'A bucket of shrimp in Times Square, followed by a Lion King matinee!'
jokes.setup: Twyla mentions the group performed in New York
jokes.punchline: Moira condescendingly offers what she assumes their 'provincial' New York experience was: 'a bucket of shrimp in Times Square, followed by a Lion King matinee'
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully specific joke. 'A bucket of shrimp in Times Square' and 'Lion King matinee' are the perfect shorthand for the tourist experience Moira is dismissing — specific enough to be vivid, accurate enough to be recognizable, and delivered with such devastating certainty. The punchline is that Twyla immediately one-ups her: they performed in Central Park. The specificity of Moira's guess is what elevates this above a generic snobbery joke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I don't want to be included.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm perfectly fine being left out. Truly. Don't mind me.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's not like I care that nobody thought to ask. I wouldn't want to go anyway.

jokes.text: Moira is visibly wrong-footed when Twyla reveals they performed in Central Park at the 'Little Towns, Big Voices' festival
jokes.setup: Moira dismisses their New York trip as tourist activity
jokes.punchline: Twyla reveals they performed in Central Park — 'C-Central Park? Really?' — Moira is genuinely caught off guard
jokes.explanation: The subversion works cleanly — the joke is in Moira's stuttered 'C-Central Park? Really?' which reveals she's genuinely impressed despite herself. The 'Little Towns, Big Voices' festival name is also a nice touch, being both earnest and slightly ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, New York. How thrilling. Let me guess—you'll be taking pictures in front of that large green lady, eating a hot dog from a street vendor, and catching a show on Broadway where you'll weep openly like it's the first time you've experienced culture.

jokes.text: Twyla mistakes Moira for being 'in like, a soap opera' when Moira declares herself a professional vocalist
jokes.setup: Moira announces she is a 'professional vocalist'
jokes.punchline: Twyla: 'Oh, really? I thought you were in like, a soap opera.'
jokes.explanation: Twyla punctures Moira's self-regard efficiently — Moira leads with 'professional vocalist' but her actual claim to fame is soap opera acting, which Twyla has correctly identified. It's a mild but effective bit of deflation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I suppose you and Twyla had some sort of... community theater experience while you were in New York? Some Off-Off-Broadway workshop?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Actually, we performed in Central Park.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I... what? Central Park? You mean... the actual Central Park? In Manhattan?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: But... that's... how did you... I don't... well, I...

jokes.text: Moira hears soap opera mention as a compliment: 'I didn't know you were a fan, thank you!'
jokes.setup: Twyla mentions the soap opera as if correcting Moira's self-description
jokes.punchline: Moira interprets it as fandom and thanks her, completely missing the point
jokes.explanation: Moira's instant reframe of a mild slight as evidence of fandom is a perfect character beat — her narcissism is so thorough that even corrections register as compliments. The brevity and specificity of 'I didn't know you were a fan, thank you!' is excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've had an extensive musical theatre background, you know. I've worked with the greatest composers. Andy Webber, of course.

jokes.text: Moira's credits: 'ensemble tracks seven, nine, and thirteen of the original cast recording of Starlight Express'
jokes.setup: Twyla asks if Moira recorded an album
jokes.punchline: Moira's answer is technically yes — she's on an album — but the specifics reveal she's on three ensemble tracks of Starlight Express
jokes.explanation: The specificity is everything here. Not just 'I was on a cast album' but 'ensemble tracks seven, nine, and thirteen.' The choice of Starlight Express (a notoriously campy, critically peculiar musical) is inspired — it confirms Moira's career is real but modest, and her pride in it is completely unironic. The beat of her trailing off and then completing 'Starlight Express' after a pause suggests even she knows how this sounds.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I had a very successful recording career. I was on an album that went gold.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, I was part of an ensemble on one track.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It was 'We Are the World.'

jokes.text: Moira's reference to 'anything Andy Webber' — mispronouncing 'Andrew Lloyd Webber' as 'Andy Webber'
jokes.setup: Moira is establishing her musical theatre credentials
jokes.punchline: She refers to 'anything Andy Webber' — a casually wrong nickname for Andrew Lloyd Webber that suggests familiarity she doesn't quite have
jokes.explanation: Catherine O'Hara's delivery of 'anything Andy Webber' is a character-specific joke — it implies Moira is on casual-nickname terms with Andrew Lloyd Webber while simultaneously getting his name wrong, suggesting the intimacy is fabricated or at least exaggerated. Deeply Moira. The joke rewards attentive viewers.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, tell her that I'm holding court at the café tomorrow at noon. She can stop by if she'd like to pay her respects.

jokes.text: 'Or perhaps Jocelyn should speak to me' — Moira's response to being told to audition
jokes.setup: Twyla suggests Moira talk to Jocelyn about joining the Jazzagals
jokes.punchline: Moira: 'Or perhaps Jocelyn should speak to me' — implying she should be recruited, not the other way around
jokes.explanation: A masterpiece of economy — five words that contain an entire worldview. The implication that a small-town choir director should be seeking out Moira rather than the reverse says everything about Moira's self-image. Perfect character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'll need a moment to block out the menu.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I find it helps if I approach each section as a different character.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The appetizers are my ingénue phase, the entrées are my Shakespearean period, and the desserts... well, that's my one-woman show.

jokes.text: Moira says she's 'not quite off book' when handed a menu
jokes.setup: Twyla hands Moira a menu
jokes.punchline: Moira accepts it saying she's 'not quite off book' — using theatre terminology for memorizing lines to describe not knowing the menu
jokes.explanation: This is a gem of a joke. 'Off book' is theatre jargon for having memorized your lines — Moira casually applies it to a café menu, revealing that her entire frame of reference is theatrical. It's so naturally delivered it almost works as sincere, which makes it funnier. Highly quotable and extremely character-specific — only Moira would say this, in exactly this way. Earns an 8+ because it's effortless, specific, and genuinely witty.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm using myself as a measuring system. It's actually quite efficient.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: You're literally just lying on top of the sweaters.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, and I'm getting very accurate measurements.

jokes.text: Mutt arriving to pick up 'cute looks for the barn' — the absurdity of fashion-coordinated farm attire for Alexis
jokes.setup: Mutt shows up at the motel room and asks for Alexis's stuff
jokes.punchline: 'Cute looks for the barn' — the concept of Alexis having curated barn-appropriate fashion
jokes.explanation: The implicit joke — Alexis has specific outfits designated as 'cute looks for the barn' — is good Alexis characterization. The shared understanding between Mutt and David ('Cute looks for... — Cute looks for the barn? — Yeah.') plays as a funny bit of resigned acknowledgment.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: What are you measuring?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The moth damage. It's a tragic story, really. These beautiful creatures have infiltrated my home and destroyed everything I hold dear.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: It's just a couple of holes in your sweater.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A couple of holes? Mutt, this is a catastrophe of biblical proportions.

jokes.text: David is discovered doing measurements with no measuring tape — using some unspecified improvised method
jokes.setup: Mutt finds David doing 'measurements' in the room
jokes.punchline: David is clearly not using a measuring tape; when asked why not, he admits he didn't think to ask Stevie if the motel had one
jokes.explanation: A solid visual gag (inferred) — David's complete unpreparedness for any practical task is confirmed by the absence of even the most basic tool. Mutt's matter-of-fact problem-solving ('Did you ask Stevie?') is a good foil. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: So, how's the chest coming along?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: *gestures to show zero progress*

jokes.text: David shows how far along he is in building the chest: 'Um, I'm this far'
jokes.setup: Mutt offers to help and asks how far along David is
jokes.punchline: 'Um, I'm this far' — clearly indicating zero or near-zero progress with a gesture (possibly just standing there)
jokes.explanation: A physical/visual punchline that lands because of its implication — 'this far' is clearly nothing, possibly just standing in the room with the wood. The economy is perfect: three words communicate total failure. Requires the visual to land fully but the transcript conveys it well.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I am available. I am ALWAYS available. My calendar is a vast, empty expanse of availability. I have cleared my schedule of all obligations, commitments, and social engagements in preparation for opportunities that may or may not materialize. I am a vessel of readiness.

jokes.text: Moira's passive-aggressive announcement: 'Small minds don't reach for the stars. No more! Not on my watch.'
jokes.setup: Moira interprets the Jazzagals not recruiting her as 'small minds'
jokes.punchline: Moira frames her self-invitation to audition as a magnanimous gift: 'Jocelyn, I am here!'
jokes.explanation: Moira reframing her own neediness as a generous act for others is perfect character comedy. 'Jocelyn, I am here!' as the climax — as if her arrival is an event — is very Moira. Good but not quite as sharp as her best moments.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: An audition? Darling, I don't audition. I am auditioned.

jokes.text: Moira describes herself as a 'trained chanteuse' and wonders aloud why a 'competitive vocal group' didn't reach out to her
jokes.setup: Moira visits Jocelyn about the Jazzagals
jokes.punchline: She describes herself as 'offer only' in entertainment — industry terminology for a star who doesn't audition — in the context of a small-town acapella group
jokes.explanation: The 'offer only' line is a great piece of industry-speak deployed in an absurd context. Moira's logic is internally consistent — in her world, she IS offer only — but applied to Jazzagals it's hilarious. High character integration because this is exactly how Moira thinks.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Annie? That's a children's musical about an orphan. I was in a production of Cats where I played the Rum Tum Tugger's understudy. We're not in the same stratosphere.

jokes.text: Moira is offended by the word 'audition' — 'In the actual world of entertainment, I'm what's known as offer only'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn says everyone has to audition, including Moira
jokes.punchline: Moira: 'Au-Audition! Though I'm sure you mean no offense... in the actual world of entertainment, I'm what's known as offer only'
jokes.explanation: 'Offer only' is real industry terminology — applying it to a community choir audition is the joke. The specificity ('in the actual world of entertainment') distinguishes Moira's world from the present context and implies the Jazzagals are beneath professional consideration. The stuttered 'Au-Audition!' before the recovery is a great performance note. Highly quotable and deeply character-integrated. Justifies near-8 craft because it introduces real industry terminology and applies it absurdly.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You know what? From now on, I'm calling you 'The Lumberjack.'

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why would you call me that?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Because you're always chopping wood and being all rugged and outdoorsy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have never chopped wood in my life. I don't even know what that means.

jokes.text: Jocelyn's defense: 'Even Gwen had to audition, and she was in a regional production of Annie'
jokes.setup: Moira objects to auditioning; Jocelyn defends the policy
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn cites Gwen's regional Annie as the ultimate credential — unaware this means nothing compared to Moira's (admittedly modest) actual career
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's earnest invocation of Gwen's regional Annie as her strongest argument is a beautifully calibrated small-town moment. She's genuinely proud of this credential. The gap between what Jocelyn thinks is impressive and what Moira's frame of reference considers impressive is the engine of the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So Mutt is teaching David carpentry. That's like asking a fish to teach a bicycle how to swim.

jokes.text: Moira's dismissive 'Who hasn't been?' in response to Gwen being in a regional Annie
jokes.setup: Jocelyn mentions Gwen was in a regional production of Annie as a serious credential
jokes.punchline: Moira: 'Who hasn't been?' — delivered as if regional Annie is a universal experience
jokes.explanation: Three words. This is possibly the most economical joke in the episode. 'Who hasn't been?' implies that Moira considers regional Annie productions so common as to be unremarkable — the funniest possible response to Jocelyn's earnest credential-dropping. The joke is pure character: it reveals Moira's world, dismisses Jocelyn's, and is deeply quotable. Economy scores a 10 — you cannot do more with three words. Earns 8+ because it's the kind of line viewers repeat to each other after watching.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: We have a specialized toolbox option. It's got all the tools you need for specific tasks.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A specialized toolbox? What kind of tools are we talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh, you know. Specialized ones.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'll take it.

jokes.text: Johnny ineffectually shouting for Bob while a customer waits — having to admit he 'can help'
jokes.setup: A customer arrives at the garage while Bob is out; Johnny shouts for Bob repeatedly
jokes.punchline: After multiple failed calls and an awkward silence, Johnny concedes: 'I can help you?' — delivered as a question
jokes.explanation: The cringe of watching Johnny desperately stall before having to admit he's the only one there is good character comedy. The 'I can help you?' as a question rather than a statement is a nice touch. Functional but not a standout.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's a cedar chest toolbox. It's handcrafted, it's vintage, it's got a felt lining, and it smells like my grandmother's closet, which is to say it smells like disappointment and old money.

jokes.text: Stevie's 'Has Paul Bunyan finished his box?' — nickname for David and his woodworking project
jokes.setup: Mutt comes to Stevie's desk asking for tools for David's cedar chest project
jokes.punchline: Stevie has already given David a nickname — 'Paul Bunyan' — mocking the idea of David as a lumberjack/craftsman
jokes.explanation: Stevie has already processed David's project to the point of assigning him a lumberjack folk hero nickname — 'Paul Bunyan' — which is a perfect image for the gap between David's self-image and actual capability. The callback nature and economy make this a solid joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, are you joking?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Maybe.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay, but where exactly would this be happening?

jokes.text: Mutt requests a miter saw, bar clamps, and dowels — tools David clearly has no idea how to use
jokes.setup: Mutt lists the professional tools needed for David's 'it's just a box' project
jokes.punchline: Stevie: 'Do you know how to use a miter saw?' — implying David certainly doesn't
jokes.explanation: A good callback to David's breezy 'it's just a box' claim — the actual professional tool list reveals how out of his depth he is. Stevie's question ('Do you know how to use a miter saw?') is asked about Mutt but clearly aimed at David by implication.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I shall perform for you all. Perhaps we could call it 'An Evening With Moira.'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Or better yet, 'Moira Rose: A Retrospective.'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm thinking 'The Moira Rose Experience: A Journey Through Time and Space.'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, no—'Moira Rose: Unearthed Treasures From A Life Well-Lived in the Arts.'

jokes.text: 'Um, no. Mutt is out back, and he's asked me to get these things for him. We're building the chest together.'
jokes.setup: Stevie asks if David knows how to use a miter saw
jokes.punchline: David admits he doesn't, immediately clarifies it's Mutt doing the work, and then claims it's a collaborative project
jokes.explanation: David's quick pivot from 'no' to 'we're building the chest together' — reframing his incompetence as partnership — is a good character beat. The dignity-saving spin is very David.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, please, take your time. I have nothing but time. And frankly, you clearly need it.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Wow, this whole thing just got a lot weirder'
jokes.setup: David reveals Mutt is helping him build the cedar chest
jokes.punchline: Stevie's deadpan assessment: 'This whole thing just got a lot weirder'
jokes.explanation: Stevie's reaction is the perfect audience surrogate line — she's registering what the viewer is thinking. The wording 'a lot weirder' implies it was already weird, which is a good compound joke. Economy is strong.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Keeping busy or hardly working?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, the expression is 'working hard or hardly working,' so unless you're suggesting that I'm either keeping busy or not employed, I'm not sure what you're asking.

jokes.text: Stevie offers David a choice between a 'basic toolbox' or a 'cedar chest toolbox'
jokes.setup: Stevie offers to get tools from the shed; David says 'Maybe? Let's go with yes, just to be safe' about needing a toolbox
jokes.punchline: Stevie straight-facedly offers the choice: 'Will you be needing your basic toolbox, or your cedar chest toolbox?'
jokes.explanation: This is a sublime absurdist bit. Stevie asks David to choose between a 'basic toolbox' and a 'cedar chest toolbox' with complete deadpan sincerity — and David immediately says 'Obviously the cedar chest toolbox!' without questioning whether it exists. Then Stevie adds the kicker: it's a big wooden box with 'tools to make a cedar chest' carved into the side of it. The layered absurdism — the fake specificity, David's instant credulity, and the fake box description — is exceptional. Earns 8+ because it builds across multiple beats and David's 'obviously' is a perfect character response.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Bob, I've been doing your job for the last three hours.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Oh! Well, thank you, Johnny. I really appreciate that.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, no, no. That's not— I was complaining, Bob. I was complaining.

jokes.text: David says 'Obviously the cedar chest tool box!' without any hesitation
jokes.setup: Stevie offers a choice between a 'basic toolbox' and 'cedar chest toolbox'
jokes.punchline: David says 'Obviously' as if this is a rational and easy choice, completely blind to the absurdity
jokes.explanation: The word 'Obviously' does all the work. It reveals David's decision-making: even when faced with a choice that doesn't make sense, he defaults to whatever sounds more curated and specific. This is perfect David characterization in a single word. Economy scores a 10 — one word containing an entire personality.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: My sales technique is to always speak with confidence and authority. When I was selling musical instruments, I'd tell clients about our finest violin.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And when they'd ask about the bow, I'd say, 'Well, that comes with the bow fiddle.'

jokes.text: Stevie reveals the cedar chest toolbox is 'a big wooden box with the words tools to make a cedar chest carved into the side of it, so it's really clear'
jokes.setup: David picks the 'cedar chest toolbox'; Stevie says it's in the shed
jokes.punchline: Stevie describes it with the additional absurd detail: it's 'a big wooden box with the words tools to make a cedar chest carved into the side of it, so it's really clear'
jokes.explanation: The third beat of this absurdist run is the payoff: the 'cedar chest toolbox' is described with enough specificity that David (and the viewer) almost believes it exists, then the description extends into self-parody ('so it's really clear'). Stevie's deadpan commitment to the bit is what makes it work — she never breaks. This extended Stevie/David exchange is the comedic highlight of the garage subplot.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's like riding a bike, Bob. You never forget.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, in this case, they're going to be riding a bike because you sold them your car.

jokes.text: David realizes Stevie is kidding — but adds 'in the off chance that you're not, where in the shed would I find that box?'
jokes.setup: Stevie has described the elaborate nonexistent cedar chest toolbox
jokes.punchline: David acknowledges she's kidding but hedges: 'in the off chance that you're not, where in the shed would I find that box?' — just in case
jokes.explanation: The perfect button on the toolbox bit. David acknowledging the joke but still asking 'just in case' is a brilliant character detail — it's simultaneously self-aware and credulous. The joke works because it tells us that David would love for there to be a specialized cedar chest toolbox, even knowing there isn't one. Excellent structure as the final beat of a three-part absurdist sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So, Mr. Turmeau, welcome to the store.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: It's Turbot. It's pronounced Turbot.

jokes.text: Moira interrupts the Jazzagals rehearsal mid-song by spontaneously singing along and then calling 'Bravo! Bravo!' at herself
jokes.setup: The Jazzagals are finishing a rehearsal
jokes.punchline: Moira's arrival mid-song with her own dramatic vocal injection, followed by self-applause, is both deeply in character and cringe-inducingly bold
jokes.explanation: The self-applause is the sharpest beat here. Moira's complete absence of self-awareness — or perhaps her total mastery of self-presentation — makes this character-specific comedy. It would be too broad with a less-developed character, but Moira makes it believable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's pronounced 'Schitt's Creek.' Schitt's. Not 'Shit's.' Not 'Skeet's.' Schitt's.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: How is this so difficult? It's a simple German name.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, no one cares how it's pronounced.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I care! I care very much. It's our name now, and I would appreciate it if people would show it some respect.

jokes.text: Moira's audition framing: 'an audition if you must, or an impromptu performance, or perhaps an evening with...'
jokes.setup: The Jazzagals invite Moira to showcase her talents
jokes.punchline: Moira offers three increasingly grandiose reframings of what is plainly an audition — culminating in 'an evening with...' (implying a full solo performance event) — each step up is funnier than the last
jokes.explanation: The escalating list structure is well-constructed. Each reframing is more absurdly self-aggrandizing than the last, and 'an evening with...' — left tantalizingly incomplete — is the perfect button. Strong character integration and the structure does real work.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: I mean, you were really struggling with it, so I figured I'd just lend a hand.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I wasn't struggling. I was... I was exploring unconventional methods of chest restoration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's called artisanal problem-solving.

jokes.text: Moira sits to watch Lena's solo — clearly a polite framing for an uncomfortable wait
jokes.setup: Moira has arrived for her audition; Jocelyn says they need to finish Lena's solo first
jokes.punchline: Moira: 'Far be it for me to get in the way of you making any necessary improvements' — a thinly veiled insult implying Lena needs a lot of improvement
jokes.explanation: Moira's 'necessary improvements' is a perfect condescension — technically supportive, actually devastating. It implies she's already assessed Lena as needing significant work. Good Moira passive-aggression.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, if this is about your feelings for me, I'm going to stop you right there. I'm flattered, truly, but I'm not interested in men who wear shirts that smell like a wet dog and a Home Depot.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: What? No, I was just going to tell you that your septic tank is backed up.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that's humiliating for entirely different reasons.

jokes.text: Johnny reveals he's been doing Bob's mechanic work all afternoon — filling tires, adding oil, and selling a car
jokes.setup: Bob thinks Johnny has his own clients; Johnny corrects him — he's been handling Bob's customers
jokes.punchline: The list escalates: filling a tire with air, adding oil... and selling a '93 Buick for $350
jokes.explanation: The escalating list of tasks builds nicely to the unexpected Buick sale, which is clearly going to be a problem. The structure (modest tasks, then a major transaction) is a good comedic escalation that seeds the upcoming conflict.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: She's like a UTI. You think it's gone, and then suddenly you're right back at the doctor's office.

jokes.text: Johnny's detailed, self-congratulatory retelling of how he sold the Buick — 'playing the guy like a bow fiddle'
jokes.setup: Bob wants to know more about the Buick sale; Johnny recounts it in the language of a master salesman
jokes.punchline: 'I'm playing the guy like a bow fiddle' — textbook sales technique applied to a situation that will turn out to be a disaster
jokes.explanation: Johnny's sales monologue is superb character comedy. The insider jargon ('slow play it,' 'hem and haw,' 'textbook stuff,' 'go in for the kill,' 'playing the guy like a bow fiddle') — delivered with complete pride and confidence — is deeply Johnny Rose. The irony (we know this is going to go wrong) is perfectly calibrated. 'Bow fiddle' for 'bow and fiddle'/'violin' is a nice mixed metaphor that also signals Johnny's slight remove from actually current idiom.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Wait, you're just telling me this because you want me to stay?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes. Absolutely. One hundred percent.

jokes.text: 'Certain instincts you just don't lose. It's like riding a bike.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has finished his proud retelling of the Buick sale
jokes.punchline: Bob: 'Funny you should use that expression — because Dick Sinson is gonna be riding his bike until we can get his car back for him!'
jokes.explanation: This is a masterclass in setup/punchline structure. Johnny's 'it's like riding a bike' is an innocent cliché; Bob's callback — 'Funny you should say that, Dick Sinson is going to be riding his bike...' — turns it into a devastating ironic reveal. The structure is perfect: the setup is innocent, the punchline recontextualizes the cliché literally and exposes the full disaster. This is professional-grade writing. Earns 8+ for the structural elegance alone.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: You can audition right now if you'd like.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Right now? Oh, well I would love to, but I'm actually coming down with something. I can feel it. It's like a flutter in my chest and a slight tickle in my throat. I think it might be... yes, I'm quite certain it's the vapors.

jokes.text: Bob: 'Dick Sinson is gonna be riding his bike until we can get his car back for him!'
jokes.setup: Johnny is basking in his sales triumph
jokes.punchline: Bob reveals that Dick Sinson didn't want to sell the car — he brought it in for brakes — and now has to bicycle as a result of Johnny's 'triumph'
jokes.explanation: This is the punchline of the extended Buick setup — the specific image of Dick Sinson riding his bike (presumably an older man, given the church donation detail) is the thing that makes the abstract wrongdoing concrete and hilarious. 'Until we can get his car back for him' implies a whole logistical nightmare that will follow. Excellent impact.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I don't think I can do this. I'm not prepared.

dialogue.speaker: Jazzagals
dialogue.line: Moira! Moira! Moira!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Stop it! This is psychological warfare. This is cruelty!

jokes.text: Bob explains Dick Sinson's car was a 'gift from the church' after his family 'hit on some hard times'
jokes.setup: Johnny sold a car that belonged to someone who brought it in for a brake job
jokes.punchline: Bob adds: it was a gift from the church after Dick's family fell on hard times — making Johnny's mistake exponentially worse
jokes.explanation: The escalation of Dick Sinson's circumstances — gift from the church, hard times — is a darkly comic reveal that piles on Johnny's mistake. Bob's delivery is kind but devastating. The comedy is in how each new detail makes Johnny's error worse.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I can't say no. I just can't do it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You literally just said no to me five minutes ago.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That was different.

jokes.text: Johnny mutters 'Ridiculous name!' about Dick Sinson, then tries to remember the name correctly
jokes.setup: Bob leaves after the Sinson revelation; Johnny is alone
jokes.punchline: Johnny mutters 'Ridiculous name!' then attempts: 'Sin-Sinson... Sinson! Dick Sin-Sinson!'
jokes.explanation: Johnny's coping mechanism — blaming the victim's 'ridiculous name' — is great character comedy. His attempt to master the name ('Sin-Sinson... Sinson!') while alone is an endearing defeat. The self-directed frustration is very Johnny.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Thank you all for being here. I'm going to perform a piece that really speaks to my soul.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's a contemporary interpretation of a woman discovering her inner power through the ancient art of interpretive dance and spoken word.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: *begins gyrating wildly while making guttural sounds* I AM THE PHOENIX RISING FROM THE ASHES OF MEDIOCRITY!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: My chakras are ALIGNED! My aura is BLINDING! I smell of EXOTIC SPICES and BROKEN DREAMS!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: *strikes an aggressive pose while humming operatically* This is my TRUTH! This is my POWER!

jokes.text: Johnny muttering to himself: 'Ridiculous name! Sin-Sinson... Sinson! Dick Sin-Sinson!'
jokes.setup: Johnny has been corrected about Dick Sinson's name multiple times
jokes.punchline: Left alone, Johnny's frustration with the name comes out — blaming the difficulty of the name ('Ridiculous name!') rather than his own failure to listen, a perfect deflection of responsibility
jokes.explanation: Johnny blaming 'Sinson' as a ridiculous name is a great character beat — his reflex is always to externalize. The physical comedy of him struggling to pronounce it while alone lands well. 'Dick Sin-Sinson' as an attempted recombination is particularly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, that was... something.

jokes.text: Mutt admits he 'can't not jump in and help when I see someone doin' something wrong' — but doesn't mean carpentry
jokes.setup: David thanks Mutt for helping; Mutt explains his motivation
jokes.punchline: David: 'Was it wrong, or was it just unconventional?' Mutt: 'No, it was wrong.'
jokes.explanation: David's attempted spin ('unconventional') and Mutt's flat refusal to accept it ('No, it was wrong') is a quick, well-landed exchange. Mutt's bluntness vs. David's euphemism is good character contrast.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I asked for space. I need to focus on myself right now.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not what Mutt said.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, David, Mutt doesn't know what he wants.

jokes.text: David's pre-emptive speech about not being romantically interested — 'If you're experiencing feelings for me, I totally get it'
jokes.setup: Mutt says he has something to tell David but needs him to promise not to tell Alexis
jokes.punchline: David immediately assumes Mutt is confessing feelings for him: 'If you're experiencing feelings for me, like, I totally get it. And normally I'd be into it, I've just been down this road before, and it's messy.'
jokes.explanation: David's complete unsolicited assumption that Mutt is confessing feelings — and his gracious, experienced management of it ('normally I'd be into it, I've just been down this road before') — is a superb character moment. It reveals David's narcissism, his complicated sexuality (he'd be 'into it'), and his genuine experience with complicated relationship situations, all in two sentences. The 'I totally get it' is particularly good — he's being generous about Mutt's nonexistent feelings. Earns near-8 for character integration and quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And he was so understanding about it. I mean, I was really worried about how he was going to take it, but he just... he took it so well.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: It was my idea.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, but like, the way that he accepted my decision to suggest his idea? Really mature.

jokes.text: David's description of Alexis: 'She sort of fades into the background after a while, you know, like a smoke alarm'
jokes.setup: Mutt is venting about Alexis always being there; David offers advice
jokes.punchline: 'She sort of fades into the background after a while, you know, like a smoke alarm' — a simile that implies Alexis is both constant noise and easy to tune out
jokes.explanation: This is a contender for the best line in the episode. The smoke alarm simile is genuinely inspired — it works on multiple levels: smoke alarms are loud, annoying, persistent, hard to ignore but eventually tuned out, and their continued presence suggests something is probably wrong. Applied to his sister by a sibling who loves her but finds her exhausting, it's perfectly calibrated. It's also deeply character-specific: only David would use this comparison about Alexis. Highly quotable. Earns 8+ for the originality and precision of the simile.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, did you actually build this?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, I did.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, Mutt helped, but I was very involved in the creative process.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So Mutt built it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We built it together. I was here the entire time.

jokes.text: Mutt asks if David is just saying that to keep Alexis away; David immediately admits yes
jokes.setup: David has given ostensibly helpful advice about living with Alexis
jokes.punchline: Mutt: 'Are you just saying that because you don't want her to move back here?' David: 'Yes, I am!'
jokes.explanation: David's instant honest admission — 'Yes, I am!' — is the ideal button on the smoke alarm joke. It strips away any charitable interpretation of his advice and confirms he's acting in pure self-interest. The honesty is funnier than a denial would have been.

jokes.text: 'She'd lose her fucking mind' — David on Alexis hearing the truth
jokes.setup: David advises Mutt not to tell Alexis any of this
jokes.punchline: 'Because no, you can't mention any of this to her, she'd lose her fucking mind'
jokes.explanation: David's casual confidence about exactly how Alexis will react is good sibling characterization. The 'fucking mind' adds appropriate emphasis and reveals the siblings' actual dynamic beneath the surface indifference.

jokes.text: Moira's audition performance — the garbled, improvisational scatting mid-song: 'It's-I-a wah!' and 'dah dah heed a woman's tears' and 'woo doo'
jokes.setup: Moira auditions for the Jazzagals with a prepared song
jokes.punchline: Her performance includes increasingly unmoored improvised syllables and vocal runs that suggest she either doesn't remember the lyrics or is embellishing dramatically beyond recognition
jokes.explanation: The performance itself is the joke — a transcript can only hint at it. Moira's vocal improvisation ('It's-I-a wah!', 'woo doo', 'boncha boom da da') suggests a performer who has made 'artistic choices' so bold they've departed from the song entirely. The character integration is exceptional because this is exactly how Moira would perform — with complete conviction and total idiosyncrasy. Strong rewatch value because the performance is visual/aural.

jokes.text: Moira suddenly decides she has 'a little bug' and wants to reschedule — moments after the Jazzagals say she's up
jokes.setup: The Jazzagals announce they're done rehearsing and Moira can audition
jokes.punchline: Moira suddenly claims illness and tries to reschedule — the transparent attempt to avoid auditioning after all her bravado is immediately recognizable as stage fright
jokes.explanation: The character reversal is the joke — the woman who was 'offer only' and needed no audition is now trying to escape the audition. Her excuse ('fighting a little bug') is transparently thin, and the Jazzagals' enthusiastic insistence ('Moi-ra! Moi-ra!') that she audition turns the tables perfectly.

jokes.text: 'That's the end.' — Moira's flat announcement after the audition
jokes.setup: Moira has concluded her wildly embellished audition performance
jokes.punchline: She simply announces 'That's the end.' — with complete professional composure
jokes.explanation: Three words. After a wild, self-indulgent performance that has presumably left the Jazzagals stunned, Moira calmly declares 'That's the end.' as if completing a professional engagement. The economy is perfect — 10/10. It's the ideal button: matter-of-fact, dignified, completely oblivious to the room's reaction. It's deeply Moira and deeply funny. One of the best single lines in the episode.

jokes.text: Jocelyn's reaction: 'Oh, that was... different.'
jokes.setup: Moira has just completed her audition
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's stunned, diplomatically chosen 'Oh, that was... different.'
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's 'Oh, that was... different.' is pitch-perfect. The pause before 'different' is doing enormous work — it's the sound of someone choosing the least damaging true word they can find. 'Different' is both accurate and diplomatic, which makes it funnier than any direct criticism would be. Economy 9.5 — the pause is part of the economy, suggesting everything that isn't said.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Thank you. Well, thank you, I'll leave you to discuss.' — exiting with full professional poise
jokes.setup: Jocelyn says 'That was... different'; Moira has to respond
jokes.punchline: Moira interprets 'different' as a compliment and exits with 'I'll leave you to discuss' — as if a formal deliberation is now required
jokes.explanation: Moira exiting with 'I'll leave you to discuss' — as if the group now faces a serious deliberation about whether to accept her — is perfectly in character. Her self-assurance is completely unaffected by the performance or Jocelyn's response. Consistent character comedy at a high level.

jokes.text: Alexis returns to the motel claiming she told Mutt she needed space — David knows this is a reversal of the truth
jokes.setup: We've just seen David and Mutt discuss how Mutt wants space from Alexis; Alexis arrives back at the motel
jokes.punchline: Alexis claims SHE told Mutt she needed her space — completely rewriting what actually happened
jokes.explanation: This is a structural joke that rewards viewers who have seen both scenes. Alexis's confident retelling inverts the actual situation — we know Mutt wanted her gone and David coached him. Alexis's self-serving narrative and David's careful responses ('That's-again, that's... You said that?') are calibrated perfectly. The irony is rich and the character integration is excellent.

jokes.text: David: 'That's excellent. Yep.' — accepting Alexis's fiction with barely concealed relief
jokes.setup: Alexis has just explained how Mutt 'took it really, really well'
jokes.punchline: David's 'That's excellent. Yep.' — clipped, satisfied, and moving on — is the comedy of someone who is thrilled by a good outcome and is trying not to show it
jokes.explanation: The button on the Alexis scene. Economy is high — 'That's excellent. Yep.' says everything about David's relief without breaking the fiction he's maintained. Works best in context.

jokes.text: Alexis compliments the cedar chest — 'That's a cute little wood thing, David' — and David accepts the compliment: 'Thank you, I made it'
jokes.setup: Alexis notices the cedar chest; David has been building it with Mutt's help
jokes.punchline: 'Thank you, I made it' — David taking full credit despite having contributed almost nothing
jokes.explanation: The callback that closes the cedar chest storyline. 'Thank you, I made it' after an episode of watching David fail at every practical aspect of construction is a perfect button. The economy is near-perfect — four words, completely in character, and it's the payoff for the entire subplot. Alexis calling it 'a cute little wood thing' is also good — zero technical knowledge.

jokes.text: Moira's post-audition debrief: 'There were definitely lots of surprised looks'
jokes.setup: Moira tells Johnny she joined the Jazzagals; he's supportive
jokes.punchline: 'Oh John, there were definitely lots of surprised looks' — Moira interprets stunned silence/shock as admiration
jokes.explanation: Moira's interpretation of 'surprised looks' as a positive response to her performance is a wonderful ironic callback. We know exactly what those 'surprised looks' were. The line also implies the Jazzagals were too polite to say anything, which tracks with their personalities. Good economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'They must've enjoyed it if they asked you to join' — his well-meaning but wrong assumption
jokes.setup: Moira says she joined the Jazzagals
jokes.punchline: Johnny assumes they must have enjoyed the audition — the viewer knows they were just being polite
jokes.explanation: Johnny's good-natured naivety is sweet but the joke is relatively straightforward dramatic irony. Works as a warm closing beat for the Rose marriage more than as a standout comedic moment.

jokes.text: Moira breaks into singing in the bedroom, and Johnny attempts to gently interrupt her twice without success
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira are settling in for the night; Moira begins singing
jokes.punchline: Moira sings increasingly nonsense lyrics ('Boncha boom da da,' 'between you and me,' 'I was older darling dream with me') while Johnny makes two increasingly urgent attempts to get her attention — 'Sweetheart.' then 'Honey?' — without success
jokes.explanation: The closing scene functions as a coda to the Jazzagals plot — Moira has awakened something she can't turn off, to Johnny's quiet dismay. The escalating 'Sweetheart... Honey?' without any effect is a nice structural echo of the episode's themes. Character integration is excellent — this is exactly who these two people are together.

tmdb_overview: Moira attempts to join the town’s a cappella group. Alexis enjoys the heady first days of her latest relationship. And Johnny helps out at Bob’s Garage despite himself.
insight: Wordplay and character escalation sustain 53 jokes across Rose family schemes.
